title: The Cloned Credential
shortDescription: A senior analyst's login was used to approve fraudulent vendor invoices after hours.

briefing.incidentSummary:
Three fraudulent vendor invoices totalling ₹4.2 lakhs were approved in the finance system of Axiom Logistics between 11:15 PM and 11:48 PM on a Tuesday night. The approvals were made using the login credentials of senior analyst Kavya Nair — who was asleep at home when they occurred.

The vendor accounts the invoices were paid to were created just 36 hours earlier and have no prior transaction history. Internal audit flagged the anomaly the following morning.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Examine login records to identify credential misuse

› Trace the vendor account creation to an internal source

› Analyse the phishing email that preceded the breach

› Collect at least 3 correct evidence items

› Flag the correct suspect

suspects.name: Deepak Soni
suspects.profileNotes: Created the two fraudulent vendor accounts from his office terminal at 3:45 PM — hours before the night approvals. The phishing email sent to Kavya Nair originated from a domain registered using an email address tied to his personal Gmail. His home IP matches the session that approved the invoices at 11:15 PM using Kavya's stolen credentials.
suspects.description: Deepak Soni is a finance executive with access to the vendor management system and invoice approval workflows.

suspects.name: Kavya Nair

digitalFootprint.locationTrace: Home — confirmed by home broadband activity

suspects.profileNotes: Victim of a credential phishing attack. She received and clicked a link in a spoofed internal IT email at 5:12 PM, which captured her login credentials. Her own home IP shows no activity after 9:30 PM. She was unaware her credentials had been compromised until the next morning.
suspects.description: Kavya Nair is a senior analyst whose credentials were stolen and used to approve fraudulent invoices.

suspects.name: IT Helpdesk Staff
suspects.profileNotes: The phishing email impersonated the IT helpdesk. However, the actual helpdesk team sent no emails after 6:00 PM on the day in question. The spoofed domain (axiom-itsupport.net) was registered externally and is not affiliated with the company. IT helpdesk logs confirm no password reset or credential activity for Kavya's account.
suspects.description: IT helpdesk staff whose identity was impersonated in the phishing email sent to Kavya Nair.

suspects.name: External Vendor (Unknown)
suspects.profileNotes: The two fraudulent vendor accounts were linked to bank accounts registered in different names. Investigation by the bank suggests these are shell accounts. However, the vendor accounts in the Axiom system were created from an internal IP — meaning an insider set up the recipient accounts. The external beneficiaries are secondary to the internal perpetrator.
suspects.description: Unknown external entity whose bank accounts received the fraudulent invoice payments.

items.label: Phishing Email → Kavya Nair — 05:12 PM
items.detail: At 5:12 PM, Kavya Nair received an email from 'support@axiom-itsupport.net' with the subject 'Urgent: Password Expiry — Action Required'. The email contained a link to a credential harvesting page mimicking the company's VPN portal. Kavya clicked the link and entered her username and password. The domain axiom-itsupport.net was registered 36 hours earlier — the same window in which the fraudulent vendor accounts were created.
items.irrelevantReason: The phishing email establishes that credentials were stolen, but doesn't by itself identify who sent it.

items.label: Domain Registration — axiom-itsupport.net
items.detail: WHOIS records for axiom-itsupport.net show the domain was registered 36 hours before the fraud using the email address 'deepak.soni.personal@gmail.com'. The registration was paid for using a UPI transaction linked to Deepak Soni's phone number. The domain was set up specifically to host a credential harvesting page targeting Axiom Logistics employees.

items.label: IT Helpdesk Outbox — No Emails After 6 PM
items.detail: The company's mail server outbox for the IT helpdesk account confirms no emails were sent by the actual helpdesk after 6:00 PM on the day in question. The 5:12 PM phishing email did not originate from any internal mail server — it was sent from an external domain designed to look like an internal address.
items.irrelevantReason: This clears the IT helpdesk but doesn't directly identify Deepak as the sender.

items.label: Vendor Management Log — Two Accounts Created 03:45 PM
items.detail: The vendor management system log shows two new vendor accounts — 'Pinnacle Supply Co.' and 'Vertex Trade Solutions' — were created at 3:45 PM from internal IP address 10.0.4.17. This IP is statically assigned to Deepak Soni's office workstation. Both vendor accounts had bank details entered immediately upon creation — unusual, as most legitimate vendor setups go through a 48-hour verification queue. The queue was bypassed using a finance executive override permission that Deepak holds.

items.label: Invoice Approval Log — Kavya's Credentials — 11:15 PM
items.detail: Three invoices for the two fraudulent vendors were approved between 11:15 PM and 11:48 PM using Kavya Nair's login credentials. The session IP is 182.64.xx.xx — a residential broadband address not associated with Kavya. Her own home broadband IP (49.37.xx.xx) shows no activity after 9:30 PM. The session device fingerprint — browser agent, screen resolution, and timezone offset — matches Deepak Soni's personal laptop profile from earlier office sessions.

items.label: Kavya Home IP — No Finance System Access After 9:30 PM
items.detail: Network logs from Kavya's home broadband ISP confirm her IP (49.37.xx.xx) last accessed any company system at 9:28 PM when she checked her work email. No further outbound connections to Axiom's finance portal were recorded from her IP for the rest of the night. This directly contradicts the possibility that she approved the invoices herself.

items.label: Session Device Fingerprint — Matches Deepak's Laptop
items.detail: Browser fingerprint data captured during the fraudulent approval session includes: User-Agent string (Chrome 120 / Windows 11), screen resolution (1920×1080), system timezone (IST UTC+5:30), and installed font set. When compared against Deepak Soni's office session fingerprints from the previous two weeks, the match probability is 99.3%. The same browser configuration was used to access the vendor management system from his office IP earlier that afternoon.

items.label: UPI Payment — Domain Registration Linked to Deepak's Phone
items.detail: The UPI transaction used to pay for the axiom-itsupport.net domain registration traces back to a mobile number registered to Deepak Soni. The payment of ₹849 was made from his PhonePe account two days before the fraud. The transaction reference and registered mobile number were obtained through a legal intercept request to the payment provider.

minigame.title: Decode the Harvested Credential Log
minigame.hint: The phishing kit stored captured credentials in a Caesar cipher shifted by 5. Decode the username to confirm whose credentials were harvested.
hints: A Caesar cipher shifts each letter by a fixed number. Try shifting each letter backwards by 5.
P shifted back 5 is K. A shifted back 5 is V. Keep going.
The decoded username belongs to the employee whose credentials were stolen and used for the late-night approvals.

onSuccess.message: Credential log decoded. The phishing kit captured Kavya Nair's username and password at 05:14 PM. Full credential record now unlocked.

hiddenItem.label: Phishing Kit Log — Kavya's Credentials Captured 05:14 PM
hiddenItem.detail: The recovered phishing kit server log shows Kavya Nair's username and password were captured at 05:14 PM — two minutes after she received the phishing email and clicked the link. The credentials were then used 6 hours later to approve the invoices. The phishing kit was hosted on the same server as axiom-itsupport.net, which was registered and paid for by Deepak Soni.

items.label: Approval Session IP — 182.64.xx.xx — Not Kavya
items.detail: The invoice approval session between 11:15 PM and 11:48 PM originated from IP 182.64.xx.xx. ISP records confirm this IP belongs to a residential broadband connection in Mumbai registered to Deepak Soni's home address. Kavya Nair's registered home broadband IP is 49.37.xx.xx — a completely different ISP and address. The approvals were made from Deepak's home, using Kavya's credentials.

items.label: Vendor Creation IP — 10.0.4.17 — Deepak's Workstation
items.detail: The two fraudulent vendor accounts were created from IP 10.0.4.17 — the static internal IP assigned to Deepak Soni's office workstation. This was done during business hours, suggesting he used his normal work session to set up the recipient accounts before executing the after-hours credential theft.

timeline.title: Fraudulent Domain Registered
timeline.description: axiom-itsupport.net registered using Deepak Soni's Gmail and paid via his UPI. Phishing kit deployed on the server.

timeline.title: Fraudulent Vendor Accounts Created
timeline.description: Two vendor accounts created from Deepak's office workstation (10.0.4.17) with bank details entered immediately, bypassing the 48-hour verification queue.

timeline.title: Phishing Email Sent to Kavya
timeline.description: Spoofed IT support email sent to Kavya Nair prompting a password reset. She clicks the link and enters her credentials.

timeline.title: Credentials Captured
timeline.description: Kavya's username and password logged by the phishing kit on axiom-itsupport.net.

timeline.title: Kavya's Last Legitimate Activity
timeline.description: Kavya checks work email from her home IP. No further company system access from her IP for the rest of the night.

timeline.title: Fraudulent Invoices Approved
timeline.description: Three invoices totalling ₹4.2 lakhs approved using Kavya's credentials from Deepak's home IP (182.64.xx.xx). Payments routed to the fraudulent vendor accounts.

updates.note: Domain registered using Deepak's Gmail and UPI. Kavya and IT helpdesk cleared.
Vendor accounts created from Deepak's office terminal.
Browser fingerprint matches Deepak's profile. Kavya's home IP not involved.
Approval session IP registered to Deepak's home address.

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Deepak Soni registered the phishing domain, created the fraudulent vendors, and used stolen credentials to approve ₹4.2 lakhs in payments from his home.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: You identified Deepak but the evidence chain is incomplete for prosecution.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: Deepak Soni orchestrated the entire fraud — from registering the phishing domain to approving invoices from his home using stolen credentials.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

coldCase.title: Case Gone Cold
coldCase.subtitle: No evidence collected. ₹4.2 lakhs was paid to fraudulent vendors and the perpetrator was never identified.
coldCase.label: COLD CASE

finalTwist.title: The Inside Job
finalTwist.description: Deepak Soni spent two days setting up the entire fraud infrastructure: registering the phishing domain, deploying the credential harvester, and creating the fraudulent vendor accounts from his own workstation. On the day of the fraud, he phished Kavya's credentials and used them six hours later — from his home — to approve the invoices he'd already prepared. The device fingerprint, UPI trail, and IP address left a complete digital signature.
finalTwist.conclusion:
✔ Phishing domain registered using Deepak's Gmail and UPI
✔ Vendor accounts created from his office IP
✔ Approval session IP matched his home broadband
✔ Browser fingerprint 99.3% match to his profile
